explanation: The book and the Nobel Prize are both mentioned, but neither is the point. The turn comes at “So, you’re going to become … what?” — from that moment the subject is Amelia herself. Note also that it is Amelia, not Chloe, who first raises studying again.
prompt: What is this conversation really about? Listen to the whole recording.
options: Amelia describes a scientist who was overlooked, and the conversation turns into her rethinking her own future.
Amelia explains why she prefers serious books to light reading, and Chloe defends her own taste.
Chloe persuades a reluctant Amelia to go back to university and study physics.
Amelia argues that the Nobel Prize should have gone to Bell-Burnell rather than her supervisor.
answer: Amelia describes a scientist who was overlooked, and the conversation turns into her rethinking her own future.

explanation: Two moves. First the challenge — “are you really prepared to study and put in all that effort?” Then the reversal, marked by “well, actually …” and closed with “I admire that.” The fourth option is tempting because the ending is warm, but it ignores the doubt that came first.
prompt: How does Chloe’s stance shift across this section?
options: From doubt about Amelia’s commitment to open admiration for the risk.
From enthusiasm about the plan to concern about how much it will cost.
From polite interest to irritation at being lectured.
She is supportive throughout and simply repeats her encouragement.
answer: From doubt about Amelia’s commitment to open admiration for the risk.

explanation: Note the hedge: probably, not certainly. The quote is encouragement, not a promise — which is exactly why Amelia finds it persuasive.
text: Be prepared to take a risk – you’ll probably surprise yourself

explanation: The whole judgement sits in the qualifier. in a quiet way reframes “determined” — not forceful, but unwavering.
answer: she was really determined, but in a quiet way

explanation: “That’s the thing, that’s what got me interested”. Her register is curiosity, not anger — the fourth option misreads the attitude.
prompt: Why does Amelia bring up the article?
options: Because the physicist’s lack of recognition struck her.
Because Chloe asked her to recommend a science website.
Because she wants Chloe to read more serious books.
Because she is angry that a man won the prize.
answer: Because the physicist’s lack of recognition struck her.

explanation: The joke is in one word: “this morning.” Options two and three invert what she says — she finds it easier than expected, and she commits to cycling even if it rains.
prompt: Why is the interviewer’s “good luck with that” quietly amusing?
options: Alison has been recommending the challenge warmly but only started it hours earlier.
Alison has admitted the challenge is harder than she expected.
Alison has chosen something that becomes impossible in bad weather.
Alison has already failed the same challenge once before.
answer: Alison has been recommending the challenge warmly but only started it hours earlier.

explanation: came away convinced — the conviction is the result of attending, not the reason she went. That sequence matters for the joke that follows.
text: I think it’s a great idea, yes. I came away convinced

explanation: much easier than I expected — the comparison is with her own expectation, and this is what makes the reveal a moment later so pointed.
answer: Really well. I’m finding it much easier than I expected

explanation: Option three swaps in a real detail from the wrong place: giving up happens after a week or two, which is precisely the problem thirty days is meant to solve.
prompt: What is the psychological basis for choosing thirty days?
options: It is roughly the time the brain needs to shift to a new direction.
It is the longest most people can stay motivated.
It is the point at which most people give up.
It is the period doctors recommend for testing a new diet.
answer: It is roughly the time the brain needs to shift to a new direction.

explanation: “I’ll do mine later” — she is postponing, not refusing. Option three reverses the roles: it is Tessa who asks about the deadline, and Becky who answers it instantly.
prompt: What does Becky’s answer reveal about her plans for the assignment?
options: She intends to do it, but only after work and a night out.
She has already finished it and is free this evening.
She has forgotten the deadline and has to ask Tessa.
She thinks the lecture has made the homework unnecessary.
answer: She intends to do it, but only after work and a night out.

explanation: Option two belongs to the wrong speaker — it is Sam who admits he still gets them mixed up, and he says it to soften her joke.
prompt: What does Becky’s reply about counting to fifteen suggest?
options: She is joking that the instruction was more obvious than it needed to be.
She is worried that she will mix up the table numbers.
She is asking Sam to go through the numbering again.
She is irritated at being given so much detail at once.
answer: She is joking that the instruction was more obvious than it needed to be.

explanation: The repetition does the work: “The wedding?” — “Yes, our wedding!” Tom has to supply the possessive before she catches up.
prompt: Why is Becky’s reaction at the end significant?
options: She has forgotten that the thing to be discussed is her own wedding.
She has realised that Tom is planning a surprise for her.
She has confused Tom with one of the café’s customers.
She has decided that the wedding should be postponed.
answer: She has forgotten that the thing to be discussed is her own wedding.

explanation: Remember to … is the softer of the two forms Sam uses for rules; the harder one, don’t touch the food, comes immediately before it.
text: Remember to always use these tongs to pick food up

explanation: “talk over a few things about the wedding”. Work and study are both mentioned in the call, but as reasons Becky cannot come.
prompt: What does Tom want to do that evening?
options: Go through some arrangements for the wedding.
Help Becky get ready for her first day at work.
Meet Becky at the café where she is working.
Study with Becky for the assignment that is due.
answer: Go through some arrangements for the wedding.

explanation: Option two is half right — only Derek is talked round, by his niece. Option three borrows Gina’s complaint and wrongly extends it to Derek, whose objection was never about work.
prompt: How do the two accounts differ in shape?
options: Gina’s judgement of a person hardens; Derek’s judgement of something softens.
Both speakers change their minds after someone explains things to them.
Both speakers complain about the way technology is used at work.
Gina describes one incident; Derek describes a series of arguments.
answer: Gina’s judgement of a person hardens; Derek’s judgement of something softens.

explanation: Option two is where the persuasion starts, not where it lands — the decision follows “I could see her point” about LinkedIn and clients. Option three happens afterwards, and he presents it as a lapse.
prompt: What finally persuaded him?
options: A practical business argument added to a family one.
Being told he was the only relative not online.
Wanting to share pictures of a new puppy.
Finding that his online banking had become less convenient.
answer: A practical business argument added to a family one.

explanation: Don’t get me wrong is a pre-emptive move: he blocks the obvious inference before making his real point.
text: Don’t get me wrong – I’m no technophobe

explanation: see someone’s point — to accept that their argument has force. It concedes the reasoning without conceding enthusiasm.
answer: I could see her point

explanation: The contrast is signalled by but and softened by to tell you the truth — a marker that what follows is the unpopular view.
prompt: How does Gina’s view of Michaela differ from her colleagues’?
options: They find her impressive; Gina finds her arrogant.
They find her inspiring; Gina finds her disorganised.
They think she is overworked; Gina thinks she is lazy.
They dislike her; Gina defends her.
answer: They find her impressive; Gina finds her arrogant.

explanation: Abby has to check what he means — “About surfing you mean?” — which tells you the shift was real enough to need confirming. Robben Island is genuinely in the recording, but forty seconds earlier and for a different reason.
prompt: Why does Rob say he will “have another think about it”?
options: Abby’s story has made him uneasy about surfing.
He has realised there will not be time for lessons.
He is joking in order to reassure Abby about her accident.
He would rather spend the time on Robben Island.
answer: Abby’s story has made him uneasy about surfing.

explanation: She frames it as received wisdom — “they say …” — not as her own conclusion, even though she has the experience to claim it.
text: if you get caught in a current you shouldn’t try and swim against it

explanation: Rob supplies the word current before Abby does. He understands what happened faster than she did at the time.
answer: You were caught in a current

explanation: Rob’s reaction is the evidence: he starts to agree, then stops mid-sentence — “watch out for what?!” The line is a joke, but it lands on top of a reassurance she has just spent thirty seconds building.
prompt: What does Abby’s final remark do?
options: It undercuts the reassurance she has just given.
It confirms that Rob should cancel the surfing.
It explains why she stopped surfing herself.
It answers a question Rob asked earlier.
answer: It undercuts the reassurance she has just given.

explanation: “I’ll definitely go to Robben Island” against “one thing I want to try …” — an intention, not a booking. The modal verbs carry the whole distinction.
prompt: Which has Rob definitely decided on?
options: Visiting Robben Island.
Booking a place at a surf school.
Going to the school Abby went to.
Staying out of the water altogether.
answer: Visiting Robben Island.

explanation: Option three is the dangerous one — the tiger really was wounded, so the inference is available. But Miles supplies his own reading: “it remembered what had happened and carefully planned the attack.”
prompt: Why does Miles stress that the tiger waited 48 hours?
options: To show the attack was remembered and planned, not instinctive.
To explain why the hunter had time to get away.
To show the tiger was too badly wounded to attack at once.
To suggest the tiger attacked the wrong man.
answer: To show the attack was remembered and planned, not instinctive.

explanation: It is a rhetorical question — he answers it himself for the next thirty seconds. The interviewer’s “What do you mean?” confirms that no information was being requested.
prompt: What is Miles doing when he says “But did they?”
options: Challenging an assumption the interviewer has just made.
Asking the interviewer for information he does not have.
Expressing doubt about whether the men actually succeeded.
Correcting a factual error about the hunt.
answer: Challenging an assumption the interviewer has just made.

explanation: The sentence works by keeping the structure identical and swapping only the pronouns. That symmetry is what makes the reversal land.
text: we’re not at risk because of tigers, they’re at risk because of us

explanation: He puts it as a question rather than a claim, which lets him make the point without having to defend it as a statement.
answer: Which is the dangerous animal, tigers or humans

explanation: Option four is close enough to be tempting — but he took part of the dead animal, which is theft, not destruction. The two-day wait belongs to the tiger, not the hunter.
prompt: What did the hunter do that provoked the tiger?
options: He wounded it and took part of its kill.
He shot it while it was sleeping in his hut.
He tracked it through the forest for two days.
He destroyed the animal it had been hunting.
answer: He wounded it and took part of its kill.

explanation: The squirrel makes option four sound right, but it is Tessa’s passing wish, not the brief. The giveaway for the real answer is the comparison with “sitting in a lecture at college.”
prompt: What is the situation?
options: Two students are doing a photography assignment outdoors.
A professional photographer is training an assistant.
Two colleagues are covering an event for their college.
A student is photographing wildlife for a competition.
answer: Two students are doing a photography assignment outdoors.

explanation: Sometimes is doing the work. Option three drops that hedge and turns a gentle observation into a rule Tessa never states.
prompt: Why does Tessa say “Maybe sometimes you don’t need to”?
options: To suggest the shot works precisely because it was unplanned.
To agree that the poor light spoiled the photograph.
To tell Becky that setting up a shot is a waste of effort.
To explain why she took her own photo so quickly.
answer: To suggest the shot works precisely because it was unplanned.

explanation: I just feel presents the judgement as a private impression rather than a claim — which makes it much harder for Tessa to argue with.
text: I just feel it’s a pretty ordinary shot

explanation: A question tag on a falling tone. She is not asking — she is inviting Becky to agree, which is how the two of them keep the mood level.
answer: Yeah, it’s hard, isn’t it

explanation: The daffodils are real, but they belong to the second half of the day — a detail from the wrong scene.
prompt: What does Tessa say she would like to photograph?
options: A squirrel.
Some daffodils.
A lecture hall.
A waterfall.
answer: A squirrel.

explanation: The invitation is built out of the complaint that came before it — the cold is the reason, so warm does the persuading.
text: My flat’s not far away. Do you fancy a nice warm cup of coffee

explanation: Option two is contradicted later — she had told the ranger, and that is what saves her. Option three is true but is a consequence she discovers afterwards, not the decision she is judging.
prompt: What is she referring to?
options: Leaving the main track for a route she thought was quicker.
Walking to the waterfall without telling anyone.
Setting out with only an energy bar and nothing to drink.
Ignoring the warning signs in the national park.
answer: Leaving the main track for a route she thought was quicker.

explanation: Option four is a reasonable guess — she does speak aloud, and she does survive. But she says the opposite about the effect: the fear stays, and only the behaviour changes.
prompt: What is the point of the contrast Luiza draws here?
options: Her outward calm was deliberate and did not reflect how she felt.
She turned out to be calmer than she had expected to be.
The bear was less frightening than she had been told.
Speaking aloud was what stopped her being afraid.
answer: Her outward calm was deliberate and did not reflect how she felt.

explanation: you here is general, not personal — she is quoting the rule she had been taught, which is why she is able to follow it while terrified.
text: I knew that you shouldn’t run away or show fear

explanation: Past perfect: by the time she says it, being lost is already an established fact she is catching up with, not something happening now.
answer: I’d got completely lost

explanation: The ranger and the overnight stay both appear later in the story — one as a person she spoke to, the other as something she was preparing for but never had to do.
prompt: Where was Luiza and what was she doing?
options: Exploring a national park on Canada’s Pacific coast.
Hiking in the Rocky Mountains with a guided group.
Working as a ranger in a Canadian national park.
Camping overnight near a waterfall.
answer: Exploring a national park on Canada’s Pacific coast.

explanation: Option two is the first half of his answer with the second half removed. Listen for But adults — well …: everything before it is a concession, not his conclusion.
prompt: What does the teacher actually say about this?
options: Each group has an advantage: children are less distracted, adults are more motivated.
Children learn faster because they are more open to learning.
Adults always learn faster because they are more focused.
Neither group has any real advantage over the other.
answer: Each group has an advantage: children are less distracted, adults are more motivated.

explanation: Option three is real pressure in the recording — “we’ve got so much to do” — but it is the problem, not the reason for this particular solution. Option four reverses the outcome: the afternoon playing is what got tighter.
prompt: Why did the band change their routine?
options: They read about matching kinds of learning to times of day.
A professional trainer told them to.
They had too little time before the tour to practise together.
They found that playing in the morning spoiled their sound.
answer: They read about matching kinds of learning to times of day.

explanation: The comparison only works one way round. A machine would suggest the opposite conclusion — that more use is always better.
text: It’s like muscles when you’re doing physical exercise – you need to rest them

explanation: doesn’t always, not never. He is removing a rule, not replacing it with the opposite rule.
answer: Learning doesn’t always need to be hard

explanation: Effort is mentioned, but as a separate point about what teachers prefer. The reason he gives for mistakes is diagnostic: “if we know what their mistakes are, we can help fix them.”
prompt: Why does the teacher say mistakes are useful?
options: They show him what needs fixing.
They prove that the student is trying hard.
They make the lesson more interesting for the class.
They help students remember the correct form.
answer: They show him what needs fixing.

explanation: Her structure is concede-then-qualify: “I think he’s right that genes are important … But I think there’s much more to it than that.” Options two and three each keep one half and throw the other away.
prompt: What is Barbara’s position on the claim?
options: Genes matter, but they are only one factor among several.
Genes are the decisive factor, exactly as the book argues.
Genes are irrelevant — it is all culture and practice.
She has no settled view because she has not read the book.
answer: Genes matter, but they are only one factor among several.

explanation: Option three inflates a real detail — she says her opponents are mostly aged 18 to 25, which is a tendency, not a rule. Watch what happens to hedges when a distractor is built.
prompt: What does she offer as evidence?
options: The players who beat her share the same physical features.
She has won fewer matches since she cut back her training.
Younger players always beat older ones.
Her own technique has got worse over the year.
answer: The players who beat her share the same physical features.

explanation: The distractors are the phrase she has just rejected. She builds the sentence by negating the assumption word for word.
text: We’re watching a competition between very different types of people

explanation: Nine words, and the whole discussion pivots on them. there’s and than that both compress heavily in speech.
answer: But I think there’s much more to it than that

explanation: Option three arrives later and belongs to Barbara, as one detail inside a much longer answer. The introduction is the book’s position only.
prompt: What does the book claim, according to Peter?
options: Many top sportspeople are that good because of their genes.
Top sportspeople succeed mainly through years of training.
Athletes born at high altitude have an unfair advantage.
Genes explain why some athletes are injured more often.
answer: Many top sportspeople are that good because of their genes.

explanation: An imperative followed by a promise. After a whole programme about genetic limits, she ends on the one factor that is under your control.
text: Start training now and you’ll be amazed at what you can achieve

explanation: Option two is Tom’s joke, offered to make Becky feel better. Attributing it to Becky reverses who says what — and misses that she is troubled rather than amused.
prompt: What is Becky’s point about Tessa?
options: Tessa seems naturally good, while Becky improved slowly over years.
Tessa has secretly been practising for years.
Tessa takes better photographs than anyone else in the class.
Becky would like Tessa to teach her.
answer: Tessa seems naturally good, while Becky improved slowly over years.

explanation: “What you might call a necessary detail?” Only the echo makes it sharp — you have to carry Tom’s wording across from the previous part of the conversation to hear it at all.
prompt: Why does Becky’s last question have an edge to it?
options: She is turning Tom’s earlier phrase back on him.
She is worried they will not be able to afford a home.
She has decided against the hotel she suggested.
She is changing the subject to avoid the guest list.
answer: She is turning Tom’s earlier phrase back on him.

explanation: A negative question used as a suggestion. It invites agreement while making disagreement possible — which is why Becky can answer “I suppose it is” without committing much.
text: don’t you think it’s a good idea to set a limit

explanation: Present perfect continuous: the point is the accumulated time, which is exactly what she thinks Tessa did without.
answer: But I’ve been taking photos for years

explanation: Nobody states the conclusion. You get “two for one”, then “that’s why I got some”, and you have to do the arithmetic yourself.
prompt: Why do they end up having cheese on toast?
options: They have both bought the same special offer.
Becky forgot to buy the chicken.
Tom does not want any salad.
They have run out of time to cook properly.
answer: They have both bought the same special offer.

explanation: Option four picks up her real self-deprecation from thirty seconds earlier and attaches it to the wrong sentence. She gives the reason immediately after the remark — the connection is it’s just something I used to do.
prompt: Why does she say she almost doesn’t consider it a sport?
options: Because it began as an ordinary way of getting to school.
Because she does not compete against anybody.
Because she trains alone rather than in a team.
Because she is not fast enough to call herself an athlete.
answer: Because it began as an ordinary way of getting to school.

explanation: so long as introduces the condition that makes the permission workable. Drop it and the rule says the opposite of what she means.
text: you can actually take three steps with the ball so long as you do it in three seconds
tokens: so long as you do it

explanation: about is easy to lose between two stressed words, and it is the difference between an estimate and a precise claim.
answer: I started about nine months ago

explanation: The cost is in his first sentence and the opening is a year behind them — both are real details placed exactly where a careless listener will grab them.
prompt: Why has the reporter come to the complex?
options: To find out how much use it is getting after a year.
To report on the opening of a brand-new complex.
To interview professional athletes who train there.
To investigate complaints about how much it cost.
answer: To find out how much use it is getting after a year.

explanation: Options two and three are each a single stage of her answer frozen in place. She raises the Harvard finding early and the disaster stories in order to qualify the first and dismiss the second.
prompt: What is Monica’s overall answer?
options: Money does not create happiness, but how you spend it does make a difference.
Money reliably makes people happier, as the Harvard study shows.
Sudden wealth usually damages people, as the well-known stories suggest.
Money has no measurable effect on happiness of any kind.
answer: Money does not create happiness, but how you spend it does make a difference.

explanation: Her summary is deliberately flat: “you just stay the same, but with more money.” Option three is the disaster story she has already called a myth.
prompt: What did that study find?
options: Winners are very happy at first but return to their earlier level after a few months.
Winners become steadily happier the longer they have the money.
Winners are less happy afterwards than they were before.
Winners are happiest if they keep the win secret.
answer: Winners are very happy at first but return to their earlier level after a few months.

explanation: Two negatives balanced against each other. Losing the second half turns a careful position into the cliché she spent the interview arguing against.
text: Money won’t make you happy, but it won’t stop you from being happy either

explanation: used to be marks a state that has ended. He never says he is cautious now — only more cautious than he was.
answer: I used to be quite an adventurous person

explanation: “The training sort of swallowed my life”. Nothing in the recording concerns money or ability — both are things you would expect to matter, and neither is mentioned.
prompt: Why did she nearly give up?
options: The training left no room for an ordinary life at her age.
She was told she was not good enough to continue.
She could no longer afford the fees.
She had been offered professional work elsewhere.
answer: The training left no room for an ordinary life at her age.

explanation: “I guess you can’t have it both ways.” He answers his own question with probably not, admits the regret, and then sets the opportunity against it — all in three sentences.
prompt: How does Fred sum up his own experience?
options: He regrets missing a normal childhood but accepts the trade.
He believes the sacrifice was not worth what he gained.
He denies having missed anything at all.
He blames the academy for what happened to Jack.
answer: He regrets missing a normal childhood but accepts the trade.

explanation: He states this immediately before the story about Jack, so the sentence works as an explanation of what follows rather than a general comment.
text: It’s a really competitive environment

explanation: I’m glad that I did — the second clause is the judgement, added from where she stands now rather than from the moment of deciding.
answer: I decided to keep going and I’m glad that I did

explanation: All four are genuinely difficult things she describes — but she ranks one of them explicitly in her opening sentence.
prompt: What does Miranda say was the hardest part of drama school?
options: Getting accepted in the first place.
The movement classes in the first year.
Deciding whether to continue after two years.
Finding an agent after graduating.
answer: Getting accepted in the first place.

explanation: The praise is specific but short — sharp detail, clear colours, “a very good first assignment” — and then he moves straight on. Nothing is asked of them retrospectively.
prompt: What does the tutor do in this part?
options: Praises the work briefly and then sets a new assignment.
Criticises the close-ups and asks them to redo the work.
Compares their photographs unfavourably with another pair’s.
Asks them questions about how the shots were taken.
answer: Praises the work briefly and then sets a new assignment.

explanation: “Oh, I don’t know, it’s not that boring” — a hedge, a partial denial, then the architecture. Tessa’s reply concedes just as carefully: maybe you’re right, I’m not sure.
prompt: How does Becky disagree with her?
options: She softens the disagreement and offers a reason to think otherwise.
She agrees completely and suggests asking for a different topic.
She states flatly that Tessa is wrong.
She avoids the subject and changes topic.
answer: She softens the disagreement and offers a reason to think otherwise.

explanation: Two moves before the acceptance: disbelief (“Me? Are you serious?”) and self-doubt. Her final I’d love to arrives only after Becky says please.
prompt: How does Tessa first respond?
options: She doubts she is good enough and has to be persuaded.
She accepts immediately and thanks Becky.
She asks how much she would be paid.
She refuses because she will be away.
answer: She doubts she is good enough and has to be persuaded.

explanation: only meant to marks the gap between intention and result — which is the entire joke, and the reason Becky adds “well, trying to, anyway”.
text: I’ve just deleted the whole chapter. I only meant to delete the paragraph

explanation: be into something — to be keen on it. She frames her advantage as a taste rather than as diligence, which makes the favour easier to accept.
answer: I’m more into the theory than you are

explanation: Three elements, all in one sentence: a bridge, a photograph, an essay. The month is a deadline, and the close-ups belong to the work he has just praised.
prompt: What exactly is the next assignment?
options: Photograph a bridge and write an essay to go with it.
Photograph any piece of architecture they like.
Write an essay about the photographs they have just shown.
Take close-up shots of wildlife within one month.
answer: Photograph a bridge and write an essay to go with it.

explanation: Option four overstates him. He says you cannot get to know the culture — and Jean, who did enjoy his year without the language, is the counter-example the recording supplies.
prompt: What is his argument?
options: Without the language you only meet people who speak English.
Learning the language is the fastest way to find work abroad.
Speaking the language makes a place look more attractive.
You cannot enjoy a place unless you speak the language.
answer: Without the language you only meet people who speak English.

explanation: Option two turns his example into a rule. He is careful to keep it personal — the beautiful town is his own, which is what stops the comparison sounding like a complaint about anywhere else.
prompt: What point is he making with the comparison?
options: How a place looks matters far less than the people in it.
Industrial towns are more welcoming than beautiful ones.
Tourists should avoid places that photograph well.
He regrets having left an attractive town behind.
answer: How a place looks matters far less than the people in it.

explanation: be supposed to against end up — a plan and what actually happened. The gap between them is the whole of his argument, delivered as a fact.
text: I was supposed to stay there for three months but I ended up staying for a year

explanation: Four words, and they close a comparison he has been building for twenty seconds. much carries the stress.
answer: People are much more important

explanation: She heads off the assumption in her second clause — “I didn’t have to go there, but I chose to.” Everything she says afterwards has to be read against that.
prompt: Why did Eva go to Toronto?
options: She chose to, because she thought it would be interesting.
Her company required her to transfer there.
She was offered a permanent job in Canada.
She wanted to improve her English.
answer: She chose to, because she thought it would be interesting.

explanation: The structure gives it away: three pieces of reassurance, then “But the bad news is …” and the lottery. Option three keeps only the comparison and drops the reassurance it was serving.
prompt: What is the speaker’s overall message?
options: The things people fear are unlikely, and the thing people hope for is unlikelier still.
Modern life is riskier than most people realise.
Air travel is dangerous but road travel is worse.
Statistics about risk are usually unreliable.
answer: The things people fear are unlikely, and the thing people hope for is unlikelier still.

explanation: He reaches it by combining two figures that were introduced separately — 1 in a million against 1 in 8,000 — which is why the conclusion sounds like a joke and is nonetheless the arithmetic.
prompt: What conclusion does he draw from it?
options: The safest journey is a train to the airport and then a flight.
You should avoid airports that can only be reached by car.
Driving is safe as long as the journey is short.
Flying and driving carry roughly the same risk.
answer: The safest journey is a train to the airport and then a flight.

explanation: almost certainly is the mirror image of the phrase he used about surviving a flight. The same hedge, pointed the other way.
text: But the bad news is, you almost certainly won’t win the lottery

explanation: unlikely carries the whole claim, and the very before it is easy to lose in connected speech.
answer: It’s very unlikely that your plane will crash

explanation: Airlines do come up, but a sentence earlier and about the odds of crashing at all — not about what happens once you have.
prompt: What does he say improves your chances in a crash?
options: Sitting at the back or over the wing, near an exit.
Choosing an aisle seat near the front.
Flying with a larger airline.
Travelling during the day rather than at night.
answer: Sitting at the back or over the wing, near an exit.

explanation: Both halves are needed. He mocks — “just kind of standing around in the cold?” — and he also asks the one question that produces her real answer: “but, I mean, why?”
prompt: How would you describe the way Joe asks his questions?
options: He teases her about the work while genuinely wanting to understand it.
He is openly dismissive and never takes the subject seriously.
He is anxious about the dangers she will face.
He is an interviewer following a prepared list of questions.
answer: He teases her about the work while genuinely wanting to understand it.

explanation: Her reply does the work with a pause and a question: “Erm … at the South Pole? No, from penguins.” Option three describes the next exchange, not this one.
prompt: What is the joke in this exchange?
options: Joe names an animal that does not live at the South Pole.
Martha pretends the penguins are more dangerous than they are.
Joe assumes the penguins are tame and Martha corrects him.
Martha admits she has never actually been attacked.
answer: Joe names an animal that does not live at the South Pole.

explanation: “I’m a scientist – I never jump to easy conclusions.” When she does answer, it is hedged twice over: in a general sense, of some sort, part of the evidence.
prompt: Why does Martha refuse to give a direct answer at first?
options: She will not draw a conclusion beyond what the evidence supports.
She has not been told what the project is looking for.
The findings are confidential until the project ends.
She thinks Joe would not understand the explanation.
answer: She will not draw a conclusion beyond what the evidence supports.

explanation: The argument is about signal, not importance. A remote place is useful precisely because so little else is happening to it.
text: the changes that take place there can tell us a lot about what’s happening on the rest of the planet
tokens: the changes that take place there
on the rest of the planet

explanation: jump to conclusions — to decide before the evidence justifies it. She adds easy, which makes it a comment on the question rather than on herself.
answer: I never jump to easy conclusions

explanation: “I’m not entirely sure, but I think …” — two hedges before the prediction. Everything she says about the job carries that grading.
prompt: How certain is Martha about her daily routine?
options: Not entirely sure, but she expects the days to be similar.
Completely certain, because she has done the job before.
She has been given a detailed schedule in advance.
She does not expect to have a routine at all.
answer: Not entirely sure, but she expects the days to be similar.

explanation: Becky develops it — “just Friday and Saturday to start with” — and Sam reports it to Emma later as “Phil had an idea today”. Being the person who improves a suggestion is not the same as having made it.
prompt: Whose idea is it to open in the evenings?
options: Phil’s.
Sam’s.
Becky’s.
Emma’s.
answer: Phil’s.

explanation: “I know what you’re thinking” — she catches the pattern before he repeats it, having just stopped him doing the same thing to the musicians.
prompt: What is Emma warning him about?
options: Using Tessa’s work without paying her for it.
Asking Becky to do work she has no time for.
Displaying photographs that will not sell.
Involving college students in the business at all.
answer: Using Tessa’s work without paying her for it.

explanation: The trouble is … and the good thing about it is … are a matched pair, and the distractors are taken from Emma’s reply. Both frames introduce the same fact from opposite sides.
text: Of course, the trouble is we’d have to invest even more money

explanation: He answers a question about his mood with a fact about the business. The ellipsis — no subject, no verb — is what makes it sound like something he has said many times.
answer: Not enough money coming in

explanation: Everything is in the delivery: the hesitation, the description instead of the name, the single “OK” once he has the answer. He asks nothing further.
prompt: Why does Phil ask Becky about her friend?
options: He is interested in Tessa and does not say so directly.
He wants Tessa to help him with his book.
He is checking whether she works at the café.
He has arranged to meet her and forgotten her name.
answer: He is interested in Tessa and does not say so directly.

explanation: The report opens by conceding improvement — “getting better, but it’s still not great” — which is easy to hear as either good news or bad depending on which half you keep.
prompt: What does the report actually say?
options: Levels have fallen over fifty years, but many cities still have high smog.
Air pollution has got worse over the last fifty years.
Smog has been eliminated in most large cities.
Air pollution is now the leading cause of illness in cities.
answer: Levels have fallen over fifty years, but many cities still have high smog.

explanation: Option two drops the hedge. The report says often, not almost all — and the difference between those two words is the difference between reporting and campaigning.
prompt: How does the report explain the increase in fires?
options: Some start naturally in dry conditions, but many are lit deliberately to clear land.
Almost all of them are started deliberately by farming companies.
They are caused by rising global temperatures alone.
The cause is not yet known to scientists.
answer: Some start naturally in dry conditions, but many are lit deliberately to clear land.

explanation: Both the date and the direction of the comparison are load-bearing. Swap either one and the sentence stops being alarming.
text: by 2050 the sea will contain more plastic than fish

explanation: Two clauses pulling opposite ways, joined by but. Whichever half you remember is the version of the story you will carry away.
answer: Air pollution is getting better, but it’s still not great

explanation: The report makes the link explicit in its last sentence, widening the story from beekeepers to a much larger group. That widening is what turns it into news.
prompt: Why do the bee numbers matter to farmers?
options: Farmers need bees to pollinate their fruit trees.
Farmers sell honey as a second source of income.
Bees indicate how much pesticide is in the soil.
Farmers are required to keep hives by law.
answer: Farmers need bees to pollinate their fruit trees.

explanation: Di abandons her tour and calls the independent half the better one; Bernie, having driven himself round Europe, ends with “Next time I go away I might try going on a tour.” Neither states the irony — you have to hold both endings at once.
prompt: What is the relationship between the two accounts?
options: Each of them ends up wanting the kind of trip the other had.
Both conclude that organised tours are the better option.
Both regret having travelled with other people.
Di enjoyed her trip and Bernie did not.
answer: Each of them ends up wanting the kind of trip the other had.

explanation: Two hedges — might and of some kind — keep it from being a recommendation. It is a man conceding a point to someone he has never met.
text: Next time I go away I might try going on a tour of some kind

explanation: much better is comparative — better than the tour, not good in absolute terms. She has already admitted the tour is where she met the friend she left with.
answer: It was much better to do things on our own

explanation: Her degree is what makes it matter — she mentions studying architecture immediately before the complaint, so the two are one thought, not two facts.
prompt: Why was the Guggenheim visit a turning point for Di?
options: It was the building she most wanted to see and she was rushed.
She got lost and the coach left without her.
The museum was closed when the group arrived.
She argued with the guide in front of the group.
answer: It was the building she most wanted to see and she was rushed.

explanation: “Yes, of course it’s a good idea, but that’s not the point.” Conceding the premise and rejecting the conclusion is his move throughout — the answer is bilingualism, not a choice between the two.
prompt: How does Professor Barnett answer the question about everyone learning a global language?
options: He agrees it is a good idea but denies that anything has to be given up.
He argues that global languages should not be taught at all.
He accepts that small languages will have to be sacrificed.
He says the question cannot be answered scientifically.
answer: He agrees it is a good idea but denies that anything has to be given up.

explanation: The distractors are the assumption he has just described as common and wrong. He answers it by replacing or with and.
text: You can keep your language and learn the big language

explanation: doing fine is deliberately casual, and it sets up the contrast in the very next sentence with the languages that are not.
answer: These languages are very big, and they’re doing fine

explanation: The other three are all real mechanisms of language loss. None is mentioned here — he names contact and substitution only.
prompt: What does he give as the reason languages are disappearing?
options: Globalisation — more contact, so people switch to bigger languages.
Governments banning the use of minority languages.
Falling birth rates in the communities that speak them.
Young people moving to cities for work.
answer: Globalisation — more contact, so people switch to bigger languages.

explanation: Her two questions — “Great?” then “Great what?” — are the evidence, and his answer is about the story, not about her at all.
prompt: Why is Tessa confused?
options: Phil is reacting to his own thought, not to what she said.
Phil has misheard her and thinks she is offering him the notes.
Phil is being sarcastic about her returning the notes late.
Phil has confused her with Becky.
answer: Phil is reacting to his own thought, not to what she said.

explanation: Two self-corrections and a vague time. She makes the invitation and then blurs every part of it, which is why Phil’s reply is equally unattached: Oh right. Yeah. Sure. One day.
text: You must tell me about it – I mean, your story … your ideas. One day
tokens: You must tell me about it

explanation: more than happy — an intensified offer. It answers Becky’s thanks by insisting the favour costs nothing, which is the same move Becky made half an hour earlier.
answer: I’m more than happy to help out

explanation: The two conversations lock together: the favour granted in the first is the reason she must refuse in the second. Neither scene explains this — you carry it across.
prompt: Why can Becky not do the bridges project this weekend?
options: She has agreed to look after the café.
She is going away with Sam and Emma.
She has not chosen a bridge yet.
She is helping Phil with his book.
answer: She has agreed to look after the café.

explanation: Listen for the tags: “didn’t we, John?”, “wasn’t he?”, “weren’t they?” Each one hands the floor to him briefly and takes it straight back.
prompt: How do Kirsten and John tell the story?
options: Kirsten leads and turns to John to confirm details.
They take equal turns, alternating throughout.
John leads and Kirsten adds corrections.
Alex questions them each in turn.
answer: Kirsten leads and turns to John to confirm details.

explanation: Present tense for the place, present perfect for her experience of it. The switch is what makes the second clause a claim about her whole life rather than about the trip.
text: It’s breathtaking. I’ve never seen anything like it

explanation: though at the end of the sentence marks a contrast with what came before. He is not disagreeing about the people — he is adding what she left out.
answer: It wasn’t very comfortable, though

explanation: Alex checks the point himself — “You mean like a gun fight? For show?” — and Kirsten confirms. The confirmation is what rules out the other three.
prompt: What did they see in the town near the desert?
options: A staged gunfight put on as a show.
A real dispute between two men.
A film being made about cowboys.
A rodeo competition.
answer: A staged gunfight put on as a show.

explanation: Richard opens by contradicting the premise — “I don’t think you have to design a city to make it a nice place to live” — and then lists everything wrong with Bangkok before saying he loves it.
prompt: How do their positions differ?
options: Daniela values a city designed for people; Richard enjoys one that grew by itself.
Both agree that cities need careful planning to be liveable.
Daniela dislikes her city and Richard likes his.
Richard argues that Bangkok should be redesigned like Munich.
answer: Daniela values a city designed for people; Richard enjoys one that grew by itself.

explanation: He concedes the label and rejects the conclusion that is supposed to follow from it. The whole account is built to make that sentence land.
text: So it certainly isn’t a smart city, but it’s very exciting to live here

explanation: Five words, and they reverse ninety seconds of complaint. But at the start of a sentence is doing all the work.
answer: But I love living here

explanation: Option three is the Dublin system, thirty seconds later. Option four is precisely what he says the technology removes — “the driver doesn’t have to stop and buy a ticket”.
prompt: What does the London system do with cars entering the centre?
options: It monitors them and charges automatically for the time inside.
It stops them entering unless the driver has a permit.
It directs them to the nearest available parking space.
It charges a flat daily fee at a ticket machine.
answer: It monitors them and charges automatically for the time inside.

explanation: Three moves in twenty seconds: work and children, then “Maybe she’s right”, then “It’ll get there. Eventually.” The concession is what makes the promise sound unreliable.
prompt: How does Rob present the situation?
options: He gives reasons, accepts his wife may be right, and promises to finish.
He denies that anything is unfinished.
He blames the engineer for underestimating the work.
He says he has decided to hire a professional after all.
answer: He gives reasons, accepts his wife may be right, and promises to finish.

explanation: I just say concedes that the phrase is a label rather than a description — he is telling you it is an excuse while using it.
text: I just say that it’s a work in progress

explanation: She introduces it with OK – I admit it, framing a hobby as a confession. That framing is what makes the rest of the account funny rather than boastful.
answer: I’m addicted to renovation

explanation: The fireplace and the paintwork are both real, and both come after — they are consequences of the first change, which is the point of the whole story.
prompt: What started Antonia’s renovation?
options: She decided the kitchen cabinet was ugly.
She found damp under the paintwork.
The fireplace had been covered up badly.
The flat needed work before she could move in.
answer: She decided the kitchen cabinet was ugly.

explanation: She says it immediately after Tom’s hesitation — “it’s a brilliant flat, um …” — and Tom responds by asking for a deadline of his own. The whole exchange is about timing, not about the flat.
prompt: What is Katie doing when she mentions another enquiry?
options: Pressing them to decide by suggesting they may lose it.
Explaining why she cannot show them a third flat.
Apologising for the flat having been seen already.
Offering to negotiate the price with the owner.
answer: Pressing them to decide by suggesting they may lose it.

explanation: The distractors are what she actually thinks, in the words she used thirty seconds earlier. Being able to hold both versions at once is the point of the lesson.
text: It’s lovely! But maybe not quite what we’re looking for

explanation: can’t be bothered — cannot summon the effort. It is dismissive but not rude, which is why she can drop it a moment later without embarrassment.
answer: I’m not sure I can be bothered

explanation: Tessa’s suggestion is perfectly reasonable — the reason it fails is a third person Becky has not mentioned until now.
prompt: Why can’t Becky simply phone Tom and say she will be late?
options: They are meeting an estate agent together.
Her phone has no signal by the bridge.
Tom is already on his way to the café.
She has forgotten where they arranged to meet.
answer: They are meeting an estate agent together.

explanation: She names traffic and then sets it aside — “Apart from the traffic” — to get to the thing she actually cares about. What a speaker mentions in order to dismiss it is not their argument.
prompt: What is Marion’s objection?
options: More people passing through will make the streets less safe for children.
The traffic will make it impossible to park near her home.
Families will move away and the community will shrink.
The shops will be too expensive for local families.
answer: More people passing through will make the streets less safe for children.

explanation: One word does all the evaluating. Progress presents a change as improvement without having to argue that it is one.
text: But a new supermarket and lots of shops – that’s progress

explanation: It’s about time — it should have happened long ago. Four words that place him firmly in favour before he has given a single reason.
answer: Well, it’s about time

explanation: The bus and the local shop are both in his answer, but as reasons the centre is unnecessary. Only one thing is offered as an alternative use for the land.
prompt: What does Kamal suggest instead of the shopping centre?
options: A green living space.
A larger local shop.
Better bus services to the supermarket.
More housing for families.
answer: A green living space.

explanation: “Should I be investing the money somewhere else?” Her student loan comes up only because Mia asks about debts — it is not part of the question she called with.
prompt: What is Sophie actually asking?
options: Whether her savings would earn more somewhere other than her current account.
Whether she should take out a loan to buy a home.
How to reduce the interest on her student loan.
Whether she is saving enough each month.
answer: Whether her savings would earn more somewhere other than her current account.

explanation: She says the same thing to Jacob as “the first step to serious saving is to get out of debt”. One principle, delivered to two callers whose situations look nothing alike.
text: Before you worry about investment, free yourself from debt

explanation: get out of debt before saving — the order of the two ideas in the sentence is the order she wants him to do them in.
answer: The first step to serious saving is to get out of debt

explanation: Paying more than the minimum is what Jacob already does, and it is not solving anything. Her instruction is to change provider, not to change behaviour first.
prompt: What does Mia suggest Jacob does about the interest rate?
options: Transfer the balance to a provider with a much lower rate.
Ask his current provider to reduce the rate.
Take out a bank loan to clear the card.
Pay more than the minimum each month.
answer: Transfer the balance to a provider with a much lower rate.

explanation: “Maybe I’m just not a very honest person” is a concession, not a defence. The justification that follows is offered as reasonable, which is a far weaker claim than right.
prompt: How does Speaker 2 present their own answer?
options: They admit they would be tempted and half-justify it.
They insist that taking money would be perfectly acceptable.
They say they would take the money and feel no guilt.
They claim they would return everything untouched.
answer: They admit they would be tempted and half-justify it.

explanation: They say it themselves before anyone else can. Naming the objection first is what lets the justification continue without sounding defensive.
text: Maybe I’m just not a very honest person

explanation: Third conditional without the if-clause — the situation is understood, so only the consequence is stated. Certainly carries the weight of the whole judgement.
answer: I certainly wouldn’t have taken the money

explanation: This is the second of two scenarios they give. The email belongs to the first, where they are a guest rather than an employee — the same person, two different roles.
prompt: What would Speaker 1 have done if they had worked at the hotel?
options: Told the manager and let the hotel deal with it.
Kept the wallet safe until the owner returned.
Emailed the owner from the hotel’s address.
Handed it to the police at the end of the shift.
answer: Told the manager and let the hotel deal with it.

explanation: The voicemail is real and it delayed him, but it is not the reason — Tom raises it as something he tried, and Becky answers it with “It’s not your fault.”
prompt: Why did they not get the flat?
options: Someone signed a contract about an hour earlier.
The estate agent never returned Tom’s calls.
They could not afford the deposit in time.
The owner decided not to rent it out.
answer: Someone signed a contract about an hour earlier.

explanation: The point arrives in three words at the end — “So you never know.” Everything before it is evidence for a claim he never states directly.
prompt: Why does Sam tell the story about the café?
options: To show that losing something first does not always settle it.
To explain why he cannot help Tom find a flat.
To warn Tom that good properties are always taken.
To suggest Tom should buy rather than rent.
answer: To show that losing something first does not always settle it.

explanation: She removes the blame before he has finished apologising. Six words, and they close the subject.
answer: Don’t worry. It’s not your fault

explanation: He answers with two words — “The bank” — and the reason only emerges in the next sentence. The cooker is what the loan is for, not who he is seeing.
prompt: Why is Sam dressed smartly?
options: He is going to the bank about a loan.
He is meeting a supplier about a new cooker.
He is having lunch with Emma.
He is being interviewed for the café.
answer: He is going to the bank about a loan.

explanation: Paul’s charge is “like a lesson on how to commit a crime”; Zoe’s defence is “like a warning to people”. Everything else they say is evidence for one of those two readings.
prompt: What is the disagreement about?
options: Whether the programme taught people how to commit the crime or warned them against it.
Whether the young woman should have been arrested at all.
Whether the old man was telling the truth about the credit card.
Whether reality programmes should be broadcast in the evening.
answer: Whether the programme taught people how to commit the crime or warned them against it.

explanation: “Hardly”, then “Yeah, I suppose so” once she names when it happened — and he moves straight on to a new complaint. Conceding a point without changing position is the shape of this whole conversation.
prompt: How does Paul respond to Zoe’s point that the presenter gave a warning?
options: He dismisses it, then concedes it grudgingly.
He accepts it immediately and changes his view.
He denies that the presenter said anything of the kind.
He argues that warnings do not belong on television.
answer: He dismisses it, then concedes it grudgingly.

explanation: He undercuts it himself in the next breath — “well, for about five minutes, anyway” — which is what the distractors would have said if he had not.
text: All these boring, ordinary people turn into famous people

explanation: She is quoting the presenter, not offering her own observation — which is why Paul can answer it with “doesn’t everyone know that?”
answer: Many thieves are very charming

explanation: The niece appears later and on the other side — she is the one who noticed. Two women in one story, doing opposite things.
prompt: What was the crime in the programme?
options: A young woman used an old man’s credit card for herself.
A young woman stole an old man’s wallet in the street.
A niece took money from her uncle’s bank account.
A carer was accused of selling an old man’s property.
answer: A young woman used an old man’s credit card for herself.

explanation: Toby will not put electronics on his skin but happily eats synthetic meat; Rosie says the patch “wouldn’t worry me” and calls the meat scary. The symmetry is never pointed out by either of them.
prompt: How are their positions arranged?
options: Each is relaxed about the technology the other finds disturbing.
Both are equally enthusiastic about all the new technology.
Rosie accepts everything and Toby rejects everything.
They agree about the technology but disagree about the cost.
answer: Each is relaxed about the technology the other finds disturbing.

explanation: She does not deny that lab meat helps. She says it is not the only thing that does — which lets her point at his car without contradicting anything he has said.
text: I think there are more ways of helping out than eating meat that a scientist has put together in a laboratory
tokens: I think there are more ways
that a scientist has put together in a laboratory

explanation: More like is how you correct a figure without calling someone a liar. The second sentence is the accusation, and it needs no softening.
answer: More like a year ago. You drive every day

explanation: The plaster is Rosie’s comparison, offered to make his objection sound unreasonable. He rejects the comparison rather than the plaster.
prompt: What does Toby object to at the start?
options: Having electronics stuck on his skin.
The cost of new medical technology.
Wearing a plaster over a cut.
Being tracked by a health device.
answer: Having electronics stuck on his skin.

explanation: “It sounded like a reasonable story, so I guess people just assumed” — plausibility doing the work of evidence, which is the point of the whole lesson.
prompt: Why does B think people believed it the second time?
options: It sounded like a reasonable story, so they assumed it was true.
It came from an official government source.
The first hoax had never been disproved.
Local newspapers reported it as fact.
answer: It sounded like a reasonable story, so they assumed it was true.

explanation: Without checking is the phrase the lesson turns on, and it comes from A rather than B — the sceptic never has to say it out loud.
text: It’s amazing people believed it was true, without checking

explanation: A blanket refusal to engage, stated before any reasons. Everything that follows is B justifying a position already taken.
answer: I don’t pay attention to any of it

explanation: The instruction to pass it on is what turns a claim into an epidemic. None of the other three appears anywhere in the account.
prompt: What did the original emails tell people to do?
options: Avoid bananas from Central America and forward the email.
Report any illness to the Centers for Disease Control.
Boil or peel imported fruit before eating it.
Contact their doctor if they had eaten bananas.
answer: Avoid bananas from Central America and forward the email.

explanation: She gets as far as “Is there another flat available around here?” — another is the word that shows she has not guessed the truth. Options two and three are what she learns in the next scene.
prompt: What does Becky work out during the journey?
options: That she recognises the area and they are going to see a flat.
That Tom has already signed for a flat.
That they are going back to the flat they lost.
That Tom is taking her to meet the estate agent.
answer: That she recognises the area and they are going to see a flat.

explanation: The build-up is deliberately dull — lease, then “another document” — and the payoff is three words plus that’s all, which is exactly the phrase he used to dodge her questions in the car.
prompt: What is the effect of Tom’s final line?
options: It turns a piece of paperwork into a second surprise.
It reveals that the flat purchase is not yet complete.
It suggests the wedding may have to be postponed.
It shows he has forgotten about the lease.
answer: It turns a piece of paperwork into a second surprise.

explanation: A quiet warning, delivered before she knows what the surprise is. The whole of the second scene tests whether she meant it.
answer: I’m not sure I like surprises

explanation: One clause, given without detail. Tom shows no interest in why — his attention is entirely on having acted quickly this time.
prompt: How did the flat become available again?
options: The other people changed their mind.
The agent found an identical flat nearby.
The owner rejected the other offer.
The other contract fell through over money.
answer: The other people changed their mind.

explanation: An hour of questions before looking at what was wrong, set against a normal doctor who gives ten minutes. The tablets are described but never evaluated.
prompt: What impressed Speaker 1 most?
options: The amount of time the doctor spent understanding them.
How quickly the tablets worked.
The scientific explanation they were given.
The low cost compared with normal treatment.
answer: The amount of time the doctor spent understanding them.

explanation: Two clauses that would normally undermine each other. Placing personal result above explanation is the position three of these four speakers end up in.
text: I’ve no idea how it works, but it certainly worked for me

explanation: What a load of rubbish — informal and blunt. It is the only judgement in the recording delivered without any hedge at all.
answer: What a load of rubbish

explanation: Skiing appears at the end, as something they can do again — a result, not a cause. The cough and the headaches belong to the other speakers.
prompt: What was wrong with Speaker 3?
options: Bad pains in the knee that stopped them doing sport.
A cough that would not go away.
Headaches that drugs could not control.
An old skiing injury that had never healed.
answer: Bad pains in the knee that stopped them doing sport.

explanation: He answers the first question with “that’s easy to answer … there’s no mystery there” and closes with “the police never found him.” Solving parts of a case is not the same as solving it.
prompt: What is Bob’s general position on the case?
options: Several parts have plausible explanations, but the central question is still open.
The whole story has now been explained by recent research.
Almost nothing about the case can be established at all.
He is confident that Cooper died during the jump.
answer: Several parts have plausible explanations, but the central question is still open.

explanation: Suggests plus might have — two hedges in one sentence. The distractors replace both with certainty, which is exactly what the evidence does not support.
text: That suggests he might have landed and then hidden the parachute

explanation: After three minutes of possibilities, the one flat statement in the interview. The one thing we do know introduces it, and it is a negative.
answer: The police never found him

explanation: Bob adds a second point immediately afterwards — nobody knows whether it was really dynamite at all. Two separate doubts about the same object.
prompt: Why was getting dynamite on board not difficult?
options: Airport security checks did not exist in that form in 1971.
He was employed by the airline at the time.
The dynamite was hidden inside hand luggage.
Security staff were on strike that day.
answer: Airport security checks did not exist in that form in 1971.

explanation: They end on the same note in different words: “I’ve managed to make a dream come true” and “most important of all, I’m my own boss.” Every regret sits before that line, not after it.
prompt: How do the two of them assess their decisions overall?
options: Both regret details of the preparation but not the decision itself.
Both wish they had stayed in their original jobs.
Louise regrets going and Terry does not.
Both say the outcome was exactly as they had expected.
answer: Both regret details of the preparation but not the decision itself.

explanation: The distractors turn a modified plan into an abandoned one. He never wishes he had stayed — only that he had overlapped the two for a while.
text: I could have worked part-time for the bank and started my business at weekends only

explanation: wish + past perfect for a regret about the past. The contraction I’d is easy to lose entirely, and without it the sentence stops being a regret.
answer: I wish I’d checked this before leaving

explanation: He never applied for the promotion — that is his regret, not his reason: “If only I’d applied for his job when it became free.”
prompt: Why did Terry decide to leave the bank?
options: He disliked the atmosphere and saw a gap in his local area.
He was made redundant when the department closed.
He was offered a better-paid job elsewhere.
He had been refused a promotion he applied for.
answer: He disliked the atmosphere and saw a gap in his local area.

explanation: “I don’t think you realise quite how good you are.” It answers her surprise rather than her thanks — and it is the only evaluative thing he says.
prompt: What does the tutor say about Tessa’s own view of her work?
options: That she does not realise how good she is.
That she is too confident about her photographs.
That she should enter more competitions.
That she needs to work harder next term.
answer: That she does not realise how good she is.

explanation: get over here means recover from the shock, not overcome an obstacle. The distractors give the line to Phil, who really does say he knew she would win.
text: I couldn’t believe it, I was so surprised. First prize! I still can’t get over it
tokens: I still can’t get over it

explanation: No here contradicts her surprise rather than answering a question. Refusing someone’s modesty is a common English move and easy to mishear as disagreement.
answer: No, you deserved to win

explanation: Nothing states this. It is built from “It’s something important”, the move to his office, and his need to reassure her before saying anything at all.
prompt: Why does the tutor tell Tessa not to look worried?
options: She has assumed that being called in means trouble.
She has failed an assignment earlier in the term.
She is late for the meeting he arranged.
She has been waiting a long time for the result.
answer: She has assumed that being called in means trouble.

explanation: “You could be” — two words, and Rosa has to ask what he means. He never withdraws the answer; he enlarges the question until the competition is only part of it.
prompt: How does Kurt answer the question she actually asked?
options: He says she could be, then reframes it as a question about her whole life.
He tells her plainly that she is not ready.
He encourages her to enter without reservation.
He refuses to give an opinion at all.
answer: He says she could be, then reframes it as a question about her whole life.

explanation: I wish I had — with the verb left unsaid, because the whole conversation has supplied it. Three short clauses, and the last one is the reason he has been talking to her for four minutes.
text: Sort of. But I never really tried. Sometimes I wish I had

explanation: might have overcome — a possibility in the past that was never tested. It is the grammatical heart of the whole unit.
answer: It’s something I might have overcome

explanation: He says he is happy being a teacher at the very end, but that is where he arrived, not where he started. The order of the two statements is what separates them.
prompt: Why did Kurt never try to become a concert pianist?
options: Stage fright — he is terrified of performing to large groups.
He was told by his own teacher that he lacked talent.
He could not afford the years of unpaid preparation.
He preferred teaching from the beginning.
answer: Stage fright — he is terrified of performing to large groups.
